subject: Math
description: Place the opposite number on a number line given one.
description_plain: Your child can find and mark the opposite of a number on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Graph integer values on a horizontal or vertical number line and graph ordered pairs of integers in all four quadrants of a coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child can graph whole numbers on number lines and plot points with integers in all four parts of a coordinate grid.

subject: Math
description: Identify/determine the quadrant from a given coordinate.
description_plain: Your child can tell which part of the coordinate grid a point belongs to based on its coordinates.

subject: Math
description: Place two opposite numbers on a number line in a mathematical context.
description_plain: Your child can place two opposite numbers on a number line in math problems.

description: Find and position rational numbers on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram when the scale is given; find and position pairs of rational numbers on a coordinate plane when the scale is given.
description_plain: Your child can find and place rational numbers on number lines and coordinate grids when the spacing is shown.

description_plain: Your child can recognize that points with opposite signs in their coordinates are reflections across the axes on a coordinate grid.

subject: Math

description: Extend the understanding of the number line to include all rational numbers and apply this concept to the coordinate plane.

subject: Math
description: Find and position rational numbers on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram when the scale is not given; find and position pairs of rational numbers on a coordinate plane when the scale is not given.
description_plain: Your child can find and place rational numbers on number lines and coordinate grids even when the spacing is not shown.

subject: Math
description: Given one ordered pair, create a reflection across the x- and y-axis.

description: Create a real-world situation to demonstrate opposite numbers.

subject: Math
description: Find and position rational numbers on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram; find and position pairs of rational numbers on a coordinate plane.

subject: Math

description: May be able to place integers on the number line (with whole-number increments), extending the counting pattern to integers.

subject: Math
description: Understand that positive and negative integers are used together to describe quantities having opposite directions or values and use them to represent quantities in real-world contexts, explaining the meaning of 0 in each situation.

subject: Math
description: Recognizes patterns and makes generalizations about characteristics of positive and negative numbers in real-world contexts (may use any rational number, including fractions and decimals).

subject: Math
description: Understand that positive and negative numbers are used together to describe quantities having opposite directions or values and use them to represent quantities in real-world contexts, explaining the meaning of 0 in each situation.
description_plain: Your child understands how positive and negative numbers work together to show opposite values and can explain zero’s meaning in real situations.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of numbers to the system of rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child builds on what they know about numbers to work with all kinds of fractions and decimals.

subject: Math
description: Students use repeated reasoning to understand algorithms and make generalizations about patterns. They construct examples and models that confirm their generalization. They develop short cuts and check for reasonableness of answers. Students ask questions such as, "How would we verify that?" and "How is this similar to patterns with whole numbers?"

subject: Math
description:
The Advanced student is able to:
  • Create nets with different dimensions that represent right rectangular prisms with the same surface area. OR
  • Compare nets with a fixed volume to determine maximum or minimum surface area.

subject: Math
description:
Multiply and divide two rational numbers and interpret products and quotients of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
  • Understand that integers can be divided, provided that the divisor is not 0. Recognize that if p and q are integers then -(p/q) = (-p)/q = p/(-q).
  • Convert a rational number to a decimal. Recognize that rational numbers can be written as fractions or decimal numbers that terminate or repeat.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide two fractions or decimals and explain what the answers mean in real-life situations, and knows that dividing by zero is not allowed.

subject: Math
description: In addition to Proficient, the Advanced student is able to multiply and divide rational numbers showing more than one strategy (e.g., as repeated addition, as repeated subtraction, on a number line, properties of multiplication).

subject: Math
description: Understand that integers can be divided, provided that the divisor is not 0, and every quotient of integers is a rational number. Recognize that if p and q are integers then -(p/q) = (-p)/q = p/(-q). Interpret quotients of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child can divide whole numbers (except by zero) and understands that the answer is always a fraction or decimal number, and can explain how dividing positive and negative numbers works in real life.

description: Use facts about supplementary, complementary, vertical, and adjacent angles in a multi-step problem to write and solve simple equations for an unknown angle in a figure.
description_plain: Your child can use facts about angles to write and solve simple equations to find unknown angles in a shape.

subject: Math
description: Given a word problem, identify which formula would be used to solve the problem (area or circumference).
description_plain: Your child can decide which formula to use (area or circumference) when solving a word problem about circles.

subject: Math
description: Identify parts of a circle (radius, diameter, center, and circumference).
description_plain: Your child can name parts of a circle like radius, diameter, center, and circumference.

subject: Math
description: The Below Basic student may be able to investigate the concept of circles.
description_plain: Your child can explore and learn about circles.

subject: Math
description: Given the formulas for circumference and area of circles, solve mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas for circle area and circumference to solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Find radius when given the diameter and find diameter when given radius.
description_plain: Your child can find the radius if they know the diameter, and find the diameter if they know the radius.

subject: Math
description: The Basic student is able to investigate the concept of circles.
description_plain: Your child can explore and learn about circles.

subject: Math
description: Given the formulas for circumference and area of circles, solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas for circle area and circumference to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Understand that π is defined by the constant of proportionality between the circumference and diameter.
description_plain: Your child understands that pi (π) is the special number that shows how the circumference of a circle relates to its diameter.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate an understanding of the proportional relationships between diameter, radius, and circumference of a circle.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the diameter, radius, and circumference of a circle are connected and change together.

subject: Math
description: The Proficient student is able to investigate the concept of circles.
description_plain: Your child explores and learns about the properties and parts of circles.

subject: Math
description: In addition to Proficient, the Advanced student is able to determine how much area or circumference changes based on a change in radius or diameter.
description_plain: Your child can figure out how the area or circumference of a circle changes when the radius or diameter changes.

subject: Math
description: Given the formulas for circumference and area of circles, solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child uses formulas for circle circumference and area to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Understand that π is defined by the constant of proportionality between the circumference and diameter.
description_plain: Your child understands that pi (π) is the constant number that relates a circle’s circumference to its diameter.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate an understanding of the proportional relationships between diameter, radius, and circumference of a circle.
description_plain: Your child shows they understand how the diameter, radius, and circumference of a circle are related.

subject: Math
description: Investigate the concept of circles.
description_plain: Your child investigates and learns about circles and their properties.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-life and mathematical problems involving angle measure, area, surface area, and volume.
description_plain: Your child solves problems involving angles, area, surface area, and volume in everyday and math situations.

subject: Math
description: The Below Basic student may be able to identify the two-dimensional figure that result from slicing a three-dimensional figure parallel to the base, as in plane sections of right rectangular prisms when given a visual representation where the cross-section is drawn.
description_plain: Your child may be able to identify the flat shape made when slicing a 3D shape like a rectangular prism parallel to its base, using a picture.

subject: Math
description: The Basic student is able to identify the two-dimensional figure that result from slicing a three-dimensional figure parallel to the base, as in plane sections of right rectangular prisms and right rectangular pyramids when given a visual representation.
description_plain: Your child can identify the flat shape created by slicing 3D shapes like rectangular prisms or pyramids parallel to their base when shown a picture.

subject: Math
description: The Proficient student is able to describe the two-dimensional figure (shape and size in relation to the base) that results from slicing a three-dimensional figure parallel to the base, as in plane sections of right rectangular prisms and right rectangular pyramids.
description_plain: Your child can describe the flat shape and its size that results from slicing 3D shapes like prisms and pyramids parallel to their base.

subject: Math
description: In addition to Proficient, the Advanced student is able to describe the two-dimensional figure that results from slicing a three-dimensional figure parallel, perpendicular, or oblique to the base as in plane sections of right rectangular prisms, right rectangular pyramids, or cylinders.
description_plain: Your child can describe the flat shape made by slicing 3D shapes like prisms, pyramids, or cylinders in different ways: parallel, straight up and down, or at an angle.

subject: Math
description: Describe the two-dimensional figures that result from slicing three-dimensional figures parallel to the base, as in plane sections of right rectangular prisms and right rectangular pyramids.
description_plain: Your child describes the flat shapes that come from slicing 3D shapes like prisms and pyramids parallel to their base.

subject: Math
description: May be able to identify the type of triangle with respect to angle measures and side measures.
description_plain: Your child may be able to tell what kind of triangle it is by looking at its angles and sides.

subject: Math
description: Draw a triangle using a variety of tools when given one angle measure or one side length.
description_plain: Your child can draw a triangle when given one angle or one side length, using tools like a ruler or protractor.

subject: Math
description: Draw geometric shapes with given conditions using a variety of tools (e.g., ruler and protractor, or technology). Focus on constructing triangles from three measures of angles or sides, noticing when the conditions determine a unique triangle, more than one triangle, or no triangle.
description_plain: Your child draws shapes using tools and can build triangles from three angle or side measurements, noticing when there is one, more than one, or no possible triangle.

subject: Math
description: Explain the conditions for a unique triangle, more than one triangle, or no triangle.
description_plain: Your child explains when given measurements will make one unique triangle, more than one triangle, or no triangle at all.

subject: Math
description: Draw geometric shapes with given conditions using a variety of tools (e.g., ruler and protractor, or technology). Focus on constructing triangles from three measures of angles or sides, noticing when the conditions determine a unique triangle, more than one triangle, or no triangle.
description_plain: Your child draws shapes using tools and focuses on making triangles from three angle or side measurements, understanding when the measurements create one, multiple, or no triangles.

subject: Math
description: May be able to compute a single dimension from a scale drawing of a simple geometric figure with a scale factor of 2, 3, 5, or 10 when given a visual representation.
description_plain: Your child may be able to find one measurement from a scale drawing of a simple shape when the scale is 2, 3, 5, or 10, using a picture.

subject: Math
description: Compute a single dimension from a scale drawing of a geometric figure.
description_plain: Your child can find one measurement from a scale drawing of a shape.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving scale drawings of geometric figures, including computing actual lengths and areas from a scale drawing.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems using scale drawings, including finding real lengths and areas from the drawing.

subject: Math
description: Identify and reproduce scale drawing(s) at different scales with respect to the dimensions of the actual figure.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and create scale drawings at different sizes compared to the real shape.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving scale drawings of geometric figures, including computing actual lengths and areas from a scale drawing.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems using scale drawings, including finding real lengths and areas from the drawing.

subject: Math
description: Draw, construct, and describe geometrical figures and describe the relationships between them.
description_plain: Your child can draw and build shapes and explain how they relate to each other.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: May be able to describe qualitatively the functional relationship between two quantities by labeling a graph where the function is increasing, decreasing, constant, linear, or nonlinear when given a word bank.
description_plain: Your child may be able to label parts of a graph showing if a function goes up, down, stays the same, or changes in a straight or curved way using given words.

subject: Math
description: Describe qualitatively the functional relationship between two quantities by analyzing a graph where the function is increasing, decreasing, constant, linear, or nonlinear.
description_plain: Your child can describe how two things are related by looking at a graph and saying if it goes up, down, stays the same, or changes in a straight or curved way.

subject: Math
description: Describe qualitatively the functional relationship between two quantities by analyzing a graph where the function is increasing, decreasing, constant, linear, or nonlinear. Sketch a graph that exhibits the qualitative features of a function that has been described verbally.
description_plain: Your child can describe how two things are related by looking at a graph and can draw a graph based on a description of how it changes.

subject: Math
description: Describe qualitatively the functional relationship between two quantities by analyzing a written real-world scenario where the function is increasing, decreasing, constant, linear, or nonlinear. Sketch a graph that exhibits the qualitative features of a function that has been described in a real-world scenario.
description_plain: Your child can explain how two things are related by reading a real-life story and can draw a graph that matches the story.

subject: Math
description: Describe qualitatively the functional relationship between two quantities by analyzing a graph where the function is increasing, decreasing, constant, linear, or nonlinear. Sketch a graph that exhibits the qualitative features of a function that has been described verbally.
description_plain: Your child can describe how two things are related by looking at a graph and can draw a graph based on a description of how it changes.

subject: Math
description: Match a function in slope-intercept form to the model of a linear relationship between two quantities.
description_plain: Your child can match an equation in slope-intercept form to a real-life situation showing a straight-line relationship.

subject: Math
description: Determine the slope and the y-intercept of a linear function given an equation in slope-intercept form.
description_plain: Your child can find the slope and y-intercept from an equation written in slope-intercept form.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that the slope is the constant rate of change and the y-intercept is the point where x = 0 from a graph.
description_plain: Your child understands that the slope shows how fast something changes and the y-intercept is where the line crosses the y-axis on a graph.

subject: Math
description: May be able to apply the concepts of linear functions to real-world and mathematical situations.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the meaning of the slope of a linear function in the context of the situation.
description_plain: Your child can explain what the slope means in a real-life situation.

subject: Math
description: Identify a function in slope-intercept form that models a linear relationship between two quantities.
description_plain: Your child can find an equation in slope-intercept form that shows a straight-line relationship between two things.

subject: Math
description: Determine the slope and the y-intercept of a linear function given multiple representations, including graphs and equations in slope-intercept form.
description_plain: Your child can find the slope and y-intercept from graphs and equations written in slope-intercept form.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that the slope is the constant rate of change and the y-intercept is the point where x = 0 from an equation and a graph.
description_plain: Your child understands that the slope is the steady rate of change and the y-intercept is where the line crosses the y-axis, from both equations and graphs.

subject: Math
description: Apply the concepts of linear functions to real-world and mathematical situations.
description_plain: Your child can use ideas about straight-line functions to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the meaning of the slope and the y-intercept of a linear function in the context of the situation.
description_plain: Your child can explain what the slope and y-intercept mean in a real-life situation.

description_plain: Your child can find the slope and starting point of a line from points, tables, graphs, equations, or words.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that the slope is the constant rate of change and the y-intercept is the point where x = 0 from an equation, graph, table, and verbal description.
description_plain: Your child understands that the slope shows how fast something changes and the y-intercept is where the line crosses the y-axis when x is zero.

subject: Math
description: Apply the concepts of linear functions to real-world and mathematical situations.
description_plain: Your child can use what they know about straight-line functions to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Apply the concepts of linear functions to real-world and mathematical situations. Analyze the meaning of the slopes and the y-intercepts of two linear functions given as a written description and justify conclusions in the context of the situation.
description_plain: Your child can explain what the slopes and starting points mean for two lines described in words and explain their answers based on the situation.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the meaning of the slope and the y-intercept of a linear function in the context of the situation.
description_plain: Your child understands what the slope and starting point of a line mean in a real-world situation.

subject: Math
description: Construct a function in slope-intercept form that models a linear relationship between two quantities.
description_plain: Your child can write an equation in slope-intercept form to show how two amounts change together in a straight-line pattern.

subject: Math
description: Determine the slope and the y-intercept of a linear function given multiple representations, including two points, tables, graphs, equations, and verbal descriptions.
description_plain: Your child can find the slope and starting point of a line from points, tables, graphs, equations, or words.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the slope is the constant rate of change and the y-intercept is the point where x = 0.
description_plain: Your child understands that the slope is the steady rate of change and the y-intercept is where the line crosses the y-axis when x is zero.

subject: Math
description: Apply the concepts of linear functions to real-world and mathematical situations.
description_plain: Your child can use straight-line functions to solve problems in math and everyday life.

subject: Math
description: Use functions to model relationships between quantities.
description_plain: Your child uses functions to show how two amounts are related.

subject: Math
description: The Below Basic student may be able to recognize the equation y = mx + b as defining a linear function, whose graph is a straight line.
description_plain: Your child may recognize that y = mx + b is a straight-line equation and its graph is a straight line.

subject: Math
description: The Basic student is able to recognize the equation y = mx + b as defining a linear function, whose graph is a straight line and sketch graphs of functions that are not linear.
description_plain: Your child can recognize that y = mx + b is a straight-line equation and can draw graphs of lines and also graphs that are not straight lines.

subject: Math
description: The Proficient student is able to recognize the equation y = mx + b as defining a linear function, whose graph is a straight line; write examples of equations and sketch graphs of functions that are not linear.
description_plain: Your child can identify y = mx + b as a straight-line equation, write examples of equations, and draw graphs that are not straight lines.

subject: Math
description: In addition to Proficient, the Advanced student is able to identify linear and non-linear functions represented by verbal or written descriptions.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between straight-line and non-straight-line functions from descriptions or writing.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the equation y = mx + b as defining a linear function, whose graph is a straight line; give examples of functions that are not linear.
description_plain: Your child understands that y = mx + b is a straight-line equation and can give examples of functions that are not straight lines.

subject: Math
description: The Below Basic student may be able to compare the domains of two linear functions each represented graphically or numerically in tables.
description_plain: Your child may be able to compare the input values (domains) of two straight-line functions shown in graphs or tables.

subject: Math
description: The Basic student is able to compare properties (intercepts, domain, and range) of two linear functions each represented graphically or numerically in tables.
description_plain: Your child can compare features like where lines cross the axes and the input and output values of two straight-line functions shown in graphs or tables.

subject: Math
description: The Proficient student is able to compare properties (intercepts, domain, and range) of two linear functions each represented in a different way (algebraically, graphically, numerically in tables, or by verbal descriptions).
description_plain: Your child can compare features like intercepts, input, and output values of two straight-line functions shown in different ways like equations, graphs, tables, or words.

subject: Math
description: In addition to Proficient, the Advanced student is able to compare properties (intercepts, domain, and range) of one linear function and one non-linear function each represented in a different way (algebraically, graphically, numerically in tables, or by verbal descriptions).
description_plain: Your child can compare features like intercepts, input, and output values of one straight-line function and one non-straight-line function shown in different ways like equations, graphs, tables, or words.

subject: Math
description: Compare properties of two functions each represented in a different way (algebraically, graphically, numerically in tables, or by verbal descriptions).
description_plain: Your child can compare two functions shown in different ways, like with equations, graphs, tables, or words.

subject: Math
description: The Below Basic student may be able to recognize the input and output values of a relation in a table or a set of ordered pairs. (Function notation is not required in Grade 8.)
description_plain: Your child can find the input and output values in a table or list of pairs.

subject: Math
description: The Basic student is able to determine that a relation represented by a table or a set of ordered pairs is a function by demonstrating each input has exactly one output. (Function notation is not required in Grade 8.)
description_plain: Your child can tell if a table or list of pairs shows a function by checking that each input has only one output.

subject: Math
description: The Proficient student is able to determine if a relation represented by a graph, a table, a mapping diagram, and a set of ordered pairs is a function. (Function notation is not required in Grade 8.)
description_plain: Your child can decide if a graph, table, diagram, or list of pairs shows a function.

subject: Math
description: In addition to Proficient, the Advanced student is able to compare relations in different forms to determine if they represent a function. (Function notation is not required in Grade 8.)
description_plain: Your child can compare different forms of relations to decide if they are functions.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a function is a rule that assigns to each input exactly one output. The graph of a function is the set of ordered pairs consisting of an input and the corresponding output.
description_plain: Your child understands that a function is a rule where each input has exactly one output, and its graph shows pairs of inputs and outputs.

subject: Math
description: Define, evaluate, and compare functions.
description_plain: Your child can define what a function is, find outputs for inputs, and compare different functions.

subject: Math
description: The Below Basic student may be able to understand that patterns of association can also be seen in bivariate categorical data by completing a two-way table summarizing data on two categorical variables collected from the same subjects.
description_plain: Your child can see patterns in data about two categories by filling in a table that shows how often things happen together.

subject: Math
description: The Basic student is able to understand that patterns of association can also be seen in bivariate categorical data by constructing and interpreting a two-way table summarizing data on two categorical variables collected from the same subjects.
description_plain: Your child can make and explain a table that summarizes data about two categories from the same group.

subject: Math
description: Use relative frequencies calculated for rows or columns to describe possible association between the two variables.
description_plain: Your child can use percentages in rows or columns of a table to describe how two categories might be related.

subject: Math
description: Construct and interpret a two-way table summarizing data on two categorical variables collected from the same subjects.
description_plain: Your child can create and explain a table that summarizes data about two categories from the same group.

subject: Math
description: The Proficient student is able to understand that patterns of association can also be seen in bivariate categorical data by displaying frequencies and relative frequencies in a two-way table.
description_plain: Your child can show and understand how often things happen and their percentages in a table about two categories.

subject: Math
description: In addition to Proficient, the Advanced student is able to understand that patterns of association can also be seen in bivariate categorical data by displaying frequencies and relative frequencies in a two-way table and justifying conclusions about the frequencies and relative frequencies of data in a two-way table.
description_plain: Your child can show frequencies and percentages in a table about two categories and explain what the data means.

subject: Math
description: Use relative frequencies calculated for rows or columns to describe possible association between the two variables.
description_plain: Your child can use percentages in rows or columns of a table to describe how two categories might be related.

subject: Math
description: Construct and interpret a two-way table summarizing data on two categorical variables collected from the same subjects.
description_plain: Your child can create and explain a table that summarizes data about two categories from the same group.

subject: Math
description: Understand that patterns of association can also be seen in bivariate categorical data by displaying frequencies and relative frequencies in a two-way table.
description_plain: Your child understands that patterns between two categories can be seen by showing counts and percentages in a table.

subject: Math
description: May be able to match an equation of a line of best fit to bivariate measurement data when given scatter plot with a line of best fit.
description_plain: Your child can match a line of best fit equation to data shown in a scatter plot with that line.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the slope in the context of the bivariate measurement data when given a scatter plot with a line of best fit and an equation.
description_plain: Your child can explain what the slope means when looking at data and a line of best fit on a scatter plot.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the slope and y-intercept in the context of the bivariate measurement data when given a scatter plot with a line of best fit and an equation.
description_plain: Your child can explain what the slope and starting point mean when looking at data and a line of best fit on a scatter plot.

subject: Math
description: Draw a line of best fit and create its equation in the context of the bivariate measurement data when given a scatter plot.
description_plain: Your child can draw a line of best fit on a scatter plot and write its equation based on the data.

subject: Math
description: Use an equation of a linear model to solve problems in the context of bivariate measurement data, interpreting the slope and intercept.
description_plain: Your child can use an equation for a straight line to solve problems with two related measurements and explain what the slope and starting point mean.

subject: Math
description: May be able to know that straight lines are widely used to model relationships between two quantitative variables. For scatter plots that suggest a linear association, identify a line of best fit.
description_plain: Your child may be able to recognize that straight lines often show relationships between two numbers and can find a best-fit line on a scatter plot.

subject: Math
description: Know that straight lines are widely used to model relationships between two quantitative variables. For scatter plots that suggest a linear association, informally fit a straight line.
description_plain: Your child understands that straight lines are used to show relationships between two numbers and can draw a straight line that fits a scatter plot.

subject: Math
description: Know that straight lines are widely used to model relationships between two quantitative variables. For scatter plots that suggest a linear association, informally fit a straight line, and informally assess the model fit by judging the closeness of the data points to the line.
description_plain: Your child can draw a straight line that fits a scatter plot and decide how well the line matches the data by seeing how close the points are to the line.

subject: Math
description: Know that straight lines are widely used to model relationships between two quantitative variables. For scatter plots that suggest a linear association, informally fit a straight line, and informally assess the model by plotting the residuals.
description_plain: Your child can draw a straight line that fits a scatter plot and check how well it fits by looking at the leftover differences (residuals) between the points and the line.

subject: Math
description: Know that straight lines are widely used to model relationships between two quantitative variables. For scatter plots that suggest a linear association, informally fit a straight line, and informally assess the model fit by judging the closeness of the data points to the line.
description_plain: Your child understands that straight lines show relationships between two numbers and can draw a line on a scatter plot and judge how well it fits by how close the points are.

subject: Math
description: May be able to interpret scatter plots for bivariate measurement data to investigate patterns of association between two quantities. Describe the linear association by direction (positive/negative) and strength (correlation).
description_plain: Your child may be able to read scatter plots to find patterns between two measurements and describe if the pattern goes up or down and how strong it is.

subject: Math
description: Construct and interpret scatter plots for bivariate measurement data to investigate patterns of association between two quantities. Describe the linear association by direction (positive/negative) and strength (correlation).
description_plain: Your child can make and read scatter plots to find patterns between two measurements and describe if the pattern goes up or down and how strong it is.

subject: Math
description: Construct and interpret scatter plots for bivariate measurement data to investigate patterns of association between two quantities. Describe the association by form (linear/nonlinear), direction (positive/negative), strength (correlation), and unusual features.
description_plain: Your child can make and read scatter plots to find patterns between two measurements and describe the shape, direction, strength, and any unusual points.

subject: Math
description: In addition to Proficient, the Advanced student is able to interpret and compare scatter plots for bivariate measurement data by comparing their association by form (linear/nonlinear), direction (positive/negative), strength (correlation), and unusual features.
description_plain: Your child can compare different scatter plots by their shape, direction, strength, and any unusual points.

subject: Math
description: Construct and interpret scatter plots for bivariate measurement data to investigate patterns of association between two quantities. Describe the association by form (linear/nonlinear), direction (positive/negative), strength (correlation), and unusual features.
description_plain: Your child can make and read scatter plots to find patterns between two measurements and describe the shape, direction, strength, and any unusual points.

subject: Math
description: Investigate patterns of association in bivariate data.
description_plain: Your child explores patterns between two sets of data.

subject: Math
description: May be able to given a graph, identify the solution to a system of two linear equations, including systems with one, infinitely many, and no solutions.
description_plain: Your child may be able to find where two lines cross on a graph and understand if there is one solution, many solutions, or no solution.

subject: Math
description: Solve systems of two linear equations in two variables with integer solutions by graphing the equations.
description_plain: Your child can solve two equations with two variables by drawing their lines on a graph and finding where they cross.

subject: Math
description: Show that solutions to a system of two linear equations in two variables correspond to points of intersection of their graphs, because points of intersection satisfy both equations simultaneously, including systems with one, infinitely many, and no solutions.
description_plain: Your child can show that the solution to two equations is where their lines cross on a graph, including cases with one, many, or no crossing points.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and solve a system of linear equations.
description_plain: Your child can study and solve problems with two linear equations.

subject: Math
description: Solve simple real-world and mathematical problems leading to two linear equations in two variables given y = mx + b form with integer solutions.
description_plain: Your child can solve simple real-life and math problems that lead to two linear equations with whole number answers.

subject: Math
description: Solve systems of two linear equations in two variables with integer solutions by graphing the equations.
description_plain: Your child can solve two equations with two variables by graphing and finding where the lines cross.

subject: Math
description: Show that solutions to a system of two linear equations in two variables correspond to points of intersection of their graphs, because points of intersection satisfy both equations simultaneously, including systems with one, infinitely many, and no solutions.
description_plain: Your child can show that the solution to two equations is where their lines cross on a graph, including cases with one, many, or no crossing points.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and solve a system of linear equations.
description_plain: Your child can study and solve problems with two linear equations.

subject: Math
description: Create and solve systems of two linear equations from a real-world context that requires simplification to write in the form y = mx + b.
description_plain: Your child can write and solve two-step problems using two linear equations from real-life situations, putting them in the form y = mx + b.

subject: Math
description: Solve simple real-world and mathematical problems leading to two linear equations in two variables given y = mx + b form with integer solutions.
description_plain: Your child can solve simple real-life and math problems that involve two linear equations with whole number answers, given in y = mx + b form.

subject: Math
description: Solve systems of two linear equations in two variables with integer solutions by graphing the equations.
description_plain: Your child can solve two linear equations with two variables by drawing their graphs and finding where they cross.

subject: Math
description: Understand that solutions to a system of two linear equations in two variables correspond to points of intersection of their graphs, because points of intersection satisfy both equations simultaneously.
description_plain: Your child understands that the solution to two linear equations is where their graphs meet, because that point works for both equations.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and solve pairs of simultaneous linear equations.
description_plain: Your child can analyze and solve pairs of linear equations that happen at the same time.

subject: Math
description: May be able to solve linear equations and inequalities with rational number coefficients that include the use of the Distributive Property, combining like terms, and variable terms on one side.
description_plain: Your child may be able to solve linear equations and inequalities with fractions or decimals by using the Distributive Property, combining like terms, and keeping variables on one side.

subject: Math
description: Recognize the three types of solutions to linear equations: one solution, infinitely many solutions, or no solutions.
description_plain: Your child recognizes that linear equations can have one solution, many solutions, or no solution at all.

subject: Math
description: Solve linear equations and inequalities with rational number coefficients that include the use of the Distributive Property, combining like terms, and variable terms on both sides.
description_plain: Your child can solve linear equations and inequalities with fractions or decimals by using the Distributive Property, combining like terms, and moving variables to both sides.

subject: Math
description: Extend concepts of linear equations and inequalities in one variable to more complex multi-step equations and inequalities in real-world and mathematical situations.

subject: Math
description: Recognize through visual comparison that a two-dimensional figure is congruent to another if the second can be obtained from the first by a sequence of at most two transformations (rotations, reflections, and translations); given two congruent figures, describe a sequence of at most two transformations that exhibits the congruence between them.
description_plain: Your child can tell when two flat shapes are exactly the same size and shape by seeing if one can be turned, flipped, or slid one or two times to match the other, and can explain the steps.

subject: Math
description: Recognize through visual comparison that a two-dimensional figure is congruent to another if the second can be obtained from the first by a sequence of three or more transformations (rotations, reflections, and translations); given two congruent figures, describe a sequence of three or more transformations that exhibits the congruence between them.
description_plain: Your child can tell when two flat shapes are exactly the same size and shape by seeing if one can be turned, flipped, or slid three or more times to match the other, and can explain the steps.

subject: Math
description: Recognize through visual comparison that a two-dimensional figure is congruent to another if the second can be obtained from the first by a sequence of rotations, reflections, and translations; given two congruent figures, describe a sequence that exhibits the congruence between them.
description_plain: Your child can tell when two flat shapes are exactly the same size and shape by seeing if one can be turned, flipped, or slid to match the other, and can explain the steps.

description: Select the translation that shows parallel lines are taken to parallel lines.

description: Select the translation that shows lines are taken to lines, and line segments to line segments of the same length.

  • Compare at least two different representations (e.g., Venn Diagram, two-way table, set notation, verbal description) of events as subsets of a sample space (the set of outcomes) using characteristics (or categories) of the outcomes, or as unions, intersections, or complements of other events ("or," "and," "not"). OR
  • Develop questions that can be answered using unions, intersections, or complements.
description_plain: Your child can compare different ways to show events, like diagrams or tables, and use words like 'and,' 'or,' and 'not' to describe how events relate, or create questions that use these ideas.

subject: Math
description: Describe events as subsets of a sample space (the set of outcomes) using characteristics (or categories) of the outcomes, or as unions, intersections, or complements of other events ("or," "and," "not").
description_plain: Your child understands how to describe events by grouping outcomes and using 'and,' 'or,' and 'not' to explain how events connect.

description:
The Advanced student is able to:
  • Support or refute claims of causation from a real-world example (e.g., newspaper, website) with the understanding that a strong correlation does not imply causation. OR
  • Research or create two sets of data and their context to demonstrate how correlation and causation could be confused. Explain the reasons for confusion.
description_plain: Your child can explain if a real-world example really shows cause and effect or just a connection, or create examples to show how these can be confused and explain why.

subject: Math
description:
The Advanced student is able to:
  • Determine the best model and justify by describing the pros and cons of the data representation for two quantitative variables on a scatter plot using alternative linear, quadratic, and exponential models. AND
  • Determine the effect of removing extreme values (outliers) on the model. Make an argument for or against removing extreme values (outliers). AND
  • Discuss appropriate use of the model to make predictions while attending to precision (correct data entry errors).
description_plain: Your child can pick the best model for data by explaining the good and bad points of line, curve, and exponential fits, decide how removing unusual points affects the model and explain why to keep or remove them, and talk about when it’s okay to use the model for predictions while making sure data is entered correctly.

description:
The Advanced student is able to:
  • Use an irregular two-dimensional slice and generate the three dimensional object created from rotating it about an axis. OR
  • Explore the slicing of commonly shaped solids and analyze the patterns you find when making different slices. OR
  • Show where to slice a cube or cylinder to get a minimum and maximum number of sides of the two dimensional cross-sections.
description_plain: Your child can explore slicing unusual flat shapes and imagine the 3D shapes made by spinning them, or find where to cut cubes or cylinders to get different flat shapes.

description:
May be able to, with assistance:
  • Informally prove, (including by measurement or inspection) theorems about triangles. OR
  • Complete proofs of theorems about triangles. OR
  • Order the steps of a proof of theorems about triangles.
Theorems include: a line parallel to one side of a triangle divides the other two proportionally, and conversely; the Pythagorean Theorem proved using triangle similarity.
description_plain: With help, your child can show why certain triangle rules are true by measuring, looking carefully, or putting proof steps in order.

subject: Math
description:
May be able to make some, but not all, formal geometric constructions using at least one tool or method with assistance:
  • Copying a segment.
  • Copying an angle.
  • Bisecting a segment.
  • Bisecting an angle.
  • Constructing perpendicular lines, including the perpendicular bisector of a line segment.
  • Constructing a line parallel to a given line through a point not on the line.
description_plain: Your child may be able to do some basic geometric constructions like copying segments or angles, bisecting lines or angles, and drawing perpendicular or parallel lines with some help.

subject: Math
description:
May be able to, given a proof and figure:
  • Identify opposite and consecutive sides or angles in a figure. OR
  • Identify and define congruent figures using symbols or given notation. OR
  • Identify and define parallel lines using symbols or given notation. OR
  • Identify and define perpendicular lines symbols or given notation. OR
  • Define supplementary angles. OR
  • Draw or identify diagonals of a polygon.
description_plain: Your child can identify parts of shapes like opposite or consecutive sides and angles, congruent figures, parallel and perpendicular lines, supplementary angles, and diagonals in polygons.

subject: Math
description: Prove theorems about parallelograms. Theorems include: opposite sides are congruent, opposite angles are congruent, the diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other, and conversely, rectangles are parallelograms with congruent diagonals.
description_plain: Your child will prove important facts about parallelograms, like opposite sides and angles being equal, diagonals bisecting each other, and that rectangles have equal diagonals.

description: May be able to identify triangle theorems. Theorems include: measure of interior angles of a triangle sum to 180 degrees; base angles of isosceles triangles are congruent; the segment joining midpoints of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and half the length; the medians of a triangle meet at a point.
description_plain: Your child can recognize important triangle facts like the angles adding up to 180 degrees, equal base angles in isosceles triangles, mid-segment lines being parallel and half the third side, and medians meeting at one point.

description:
The Below Basic student may be able to perform one of the following:
  • Represent transformations in the plane using, e.g., transparencies and geometry software.
  • Describe transformations as functions that take points in the plane as inputs and give other points as outputs.
  • Compare transformations that preserve distance and angle to those that do not (e.g., translation versus horizontal stretch).
description_plain: Your child may be able to show transformations using tools like transparencies or software, describe them as functions, or compare which transformations keep shapes the same size and angle.

description:
The Advanced student is able to:
  • Explain how exponential and linear function values differ as x approaches positive or negative infinity. AND
  • Relate algebraic representations of linear and exponential functions to the explicit and recursive forms of arithmetic and geometric sequences, respectively. AND/OR
  • Create a real-world scenario and develop linear or exponential tables or graphs representing the scenario.
description_plain: Your child can explain how values of exponential and linear functions behave when x gets very large or very small, connect these functions to sequences, and create real-life examples with tables or graphs.

subject: Math
description: Write an expression for the inverse of a simple, invertible function f(x). Understand that an inverse function can be obtained by expressing the dependent variable of one function as the independent variable of another, as f and g are inverse functions, if and only if, f(x) = y and g(y) = x, for all values of x in the domain of f and all values of y in the domain of g.
description_plain: Your child can write the formula for the reverse of a simple function and understand that reversing swaps inputs and outputs.

subject: Math
description:
The Advanced student is able to:
  • Write the equation for a transformed parent function given the graph or verbal description of the transformations. OR
  • Write a description of the transformations using function notation given a verbal description of the transformations of f(x) or the original f(x) graph and its transformed graph. OR
  • Generalize effects of a transformation on domains and ranges, including effects on ordered pairs, when exploring all of the different types of transformations in multiple representations of f(x).
description_plain: Your child can write equations or descriptions for changed functions based on graphs or words, and understand how these changes affect the function's inputs and outputs.

subject: Math
description:
(+) The Advanced student is able to:
  • Compare the graphical representation, tabular representation, explicit and recursive formulas, and contextual representation for arithmetic and geometric sequences. Draw parallels to linear and exponential functions, respectively. AND/OR
  • Describe a real-world situation that can be modeled linearly or exponentially and develop the explicit or recursive formulas to model the situation.
description_plain: Your child can compare different ways to show number sequences, like graphs, tables, and formulas, and explain how these relate to straight-line or exponential growth; they can also describe real-life situations that fit these patterns and write formulas for them.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a function from one set (called the domain) to another set (called the range) assigns to each element of the domain exactly one element of the range. If f is a function and x is an element of its domain, then f(x) denotes the output of f corresponding to the input x. The graph of f is the graph of the equation y = f(x).
description_plain: Your child understands that a function pairs each input with exactly one output, and that writing f(x) means the output for input x; the graph of the function shows this relationship.

subject: Math
description:
The Advanced student is able to:
  • Write a system of linear inequalities that includes a set of specified points in a region and explain the choice of strict inequality or non-strict inequality notation. OR
  • Create a system of linear inequalities that will optimize the solution when given a real-life scenario. OR
  • Write the inequalities that describe the boundaries of that scenario and discuss the optimal solution when given a graphical representation of a real-life scenario.
description_plain: Your child can write inequalities that include certain points, explain why they use strict or non-strict signs, create inequalities to solve real-life problems, and talk about the best solutions using graphs.

description: May be able to explain why the x-coordinates of the points where the graphs of the equations y = f(x) and y = g(x) intersect are the solutions of the equation f(x) = g(x); find the solutions approximately, e.g., using technology to graph the functions or make tables of values. Include cases where f(x) and/or g(x) are linear, quadratic, and exponential.
description_plain: Your child may be able to explain why the x-values where two graphs cross solve the equation where the two functions are equal, and can find these solutions approximately using graphs or tables, including for lines, curves, and exponential functions.

subject: Math
description:
The Advanced student is able to:
  • Critique different methods used by self and others for solving quadratic equations in one variable. OR
  • Create and solve a real-world quadratic equation in one variable, determine its viable domain and solution. OR
  • Explain the purpose for the method chosen to solve the quadratic equation in a real-world situation.
description_plain: Your child can explain and compare different ways to solve quadratic equations, create and solve real-life problems using quadratic equations, and explain why they chose a certain method.

  • Graph equations from a real-world context of the form y = abx where a is a real number and b is greater than 0.

subject: Math
description:
May be able to:
  • Match a rational expression in the form a(x)/b(x) to its equivalent form q(x) + r(x)/b(x). OR
  • Rewrite simple rational expressions in different forms; write a(x)/b(x) in the form q(x) + r(x)/b(x), where a(x), b(x), q(x), and r(x) are polynomials with the degree of r(x) less than the degree of b(x) using inspection or long division where a(x) is a quadratic and b(x) is linear with assistance.
description_plain: Your child may be able to change a rational expression into a mixed form with a whole part and a fraction part, sometimes with help.

subject: Math
description: Rewrite simple rational expressions in different forms; write a(x)/b(x) in the form q(x) + r(x)/b(x), where a(x), b(x), q(x), and r(x) are polynomials with the degree of r(x) less than the degree of b(x) using inspection, long division, or, for the more complicated examples, a computer algebra system. description:
The Basic student is able to:
  • Explain why the sum or product of rational numbers is rational. OR
  • That the sum of a rational number and an irrational number is irrational. OR
  • That the product of a nonzero rational number and an irrational number is irrational.
description_plain: Your child can explain one of these: why adding or multiplying rational numbers stays rational; or why adding a rational and irrational number is irrational; or why multiplying a nonzero rational number by an irrational number is irrational.
